Metrics: {"retweet_count":3,"reply_count":0,"like_count":32,"quote_count":1,"bookmark_count":67,"impression_count":3791}
Article content: {"title":"The DeepThink Skill: How I Make AI Agents Slow Down Before Expensive Decisions","plain_text":"Most AI agents have one dangerous default:
They move.
That is the whole pitch, right? Give the agent a task, let it call tools, write code, search docs, open files, edit things, test things, and keep going.
Most of the time, that is exactly what you want.
But some tasks should not start with motion. They should start with judgment.
If an agent is about to choose an architecture, rewrite a working system, change pricing, debug a recurring failure, touch security-sensitive code, or commit a public launch direction, the first plausible answer is often the most expensive one.
So I built a Hermes skill called DeepThink.
DeepThink is not a model. It is not a plugin. It is not magic.
It is a reusable operating mode that tells an agent:
Slow down. Restate the real decision. Separate facts from guesses. Generate options. Red-team them. Score the tradeoffs. Recommend one path. End with the next safe step.
The point is not longer answers.
The point is better judgment.
Why Agents Need A Slow Mode
A good agent should not behave the same way on every task.
Some work is low-risk:
- rename this file
- summarize this doc
- fix this obvious typo
- run the formatter
- find where this function is used
For that kind of work, speed is the feature.
But other work has blast radius:
- Should we rebuild this service or patch it?
- Should we migrate databases before launch?
- Should we use one backend or split into separate services?
- Is this bug caused by storage, sync, auth, or UI state?
- Should this agent get access to production credentials?
- Should we automate this workflow or keep a human approval step?
On those tasks, speed can become confidence theater.
The agent sounds sure because language models are good at sounding sure. But underneath the polished answer, it may be mixing verified facts, assumptions, guesses, and vibes.
DeepThink exists for those moments.
It gives the agent a structured way to pause before execution.
 
The Core Rule
DeepThink has one job:
 Convert vague pressure into a grounded recommendation with evidence, tradeoffs, and the next safe step.
That phrase matters.
Not a 4,000-word essay.
Not a list of every possible path.
Not a philosophical meditation on uncertainty.
A grounded recommendation.
The agent should still decide. It should just decide after doing the work.
 
The DeepThink Loop
The workflow is simple:
1. Restate the real decision.
2. Identify assumptions.
3. Separate known facts from unknowns.
4. Gather evidence if tools are available.
5. Generate 2 to 4 viable paths.
6. Red-team each path.
7. Score impact, risk, reversibility, timing fit, and confidence.
8. Recommend one path.
9. End with the next safe step.
The loop idea was partly inspired by [OpenMythos](https://github.com/kyegomez/OpenMythos) and the broader idea of recurrent or looped reasoning: keep bringing the original problem back into the process so the reasoning does not drift.
DeepThink applies that idea at the agent workflow level.
The agent does not just "think harder." It keeps returning to the decision and asking:
- What do we actually know?
- What are we assuming?
- What would make this fail?
- Which choice is easiest to reverse?
- What is the next safe step?
That last question is the whole thing.
Example 1: Architecture
Prompt:
Use DeepThink. We are building a small SaaS. Should we use Next.js API routes, a separate backend service, or serverless functions?
A normal agent might jump straight to a stack recommendation.
DeepThink should slow down and frame the decision:
- What are the latency requirements?
- Do we need background jobs?
- Is real-time a core feature or a nice-to-have?
- Who will maintain this?
- What is the cost of changing later?
Then it might produce options:
- Option A: Keep everything in Next.js API routes.
- Option B: Split the backend into a separate service.
- Option C: Start simple in Next.js, isolate only the risky endpoints.
The recommendation might be:
Start with Next.js API routes unless there is a concrete requirement that breaks that model. Keep the domain boundary clean so a future split is possible. Do not pay the complexity tax before the product proves it needs the architecture.
That is useful.
It is not just "use Next.js" or "microservices scale better."
It ties the choice to risk, timing, reversibility, and maintenance.
 
Example 2: Debugging
Prompt:
Use DeepThink. Our app sometimes loses user sessions. Do not patch anything yet. Find the safest investigation path.
This is where DeepThink shines.
A fast agent might edit auth code, refresh token logic, session storage, and middleware in the same run.
That feels productive.
It is also how you create three new bugs before finding the first one.
DeepThink should ask:
- Is the session actually lost, or is the UI stale?
- Is the token expired, missing, malformed, or not being refreshed?
- Does it happen after reload, sleep, network loss, deploy, or tab switching?
- Are logs available?
- Can we reproduce it?
- What is the smallest test that distinguishes storage failure from rendering failure?
The next safe step might be:
Add instrumentation around session read/write and reproduce the failure before changing auth behavior.
That one sentence can save hours.
 
Example 3: Security
Prompt:
Use DeepThink. Should we let the agent run production database commands if a human asks from Discord?
A normal answer might be "yes, with permissions" or "no, too dangerous."
DeepThink should make the trust boundary explicit:
- Who is allowed to ask?
- How is identity verified?
- Are commands read-only or write-capable?
- Is there an approval step?
- Are secrets exposed to the agent?
- Is every action logged?
- Can the command be replayed or rolled back?
The recommendation might be:
Allow read-only diagnostics through Discord. Require a separate approval step for writes. Keep destructive operations behind a local operator confirmation until audit logging, identity checks, and rollback paths are proven.
That is the difference between an agent that feels powerful and an agent you can actually trust.
 
Example 4: Product Scope
Prompt:
Use DeepThink. We are two weeks from launch. Should we add onboarding, analytics, or a team workspace feature?
The agent should not just rank features by excitement.
It should ask:
- What blocks activation?
- What proves users get value
- What reduces support load?
- What is reversible after launch?
- What can be shipped without changing the core data model?
In many cases the answer is:
Ship onboarding first, add lightweight analytics second, defer team workspaces until single-user activation is proven.
Why?
Because onboarding improves the first session. Analytics tells you what happened. Team workspaces multiply complexity across permissions, billing, invites, roles, and collaboration state.
DeepThink makes that tradeoff visible.
 
The Most Important Dimension: Reversibility
The highest leverage question in the whole skill is:
How hard is this to undo?
If a decision is easy to reverse, move faster.
If a decision is hard to reverse, slow down.

This applies everywhere:

- A copy change is reversible.
- A database schema change is less reversible.
- A public pricing change is less reversible.
- A security permission model is much less reversible.
- A rewrite that replaces a working system can become a tar pit.
Good agents should understand that difference.
DeepThink gives them a ritual for it.
 
What DeepThink Is Not
DeepThink is not for every task.
If you use it constantly, your agent becomes slow and annoying.

You do not need a red-team matrix to fix a typo.
You do not need a scorecard to rename a variable.
You do not need a convergence loop to answer "what time is it?"
The skill is for moments where the cost of being wrong is higher than the cost of thinking.
That is the bar.
 
The Output I Want
When DeepThink runs well, the output has this shape:
- Decision
- Known facts
- Unknowns
- Options
- Red-team
- Scorecard
- Recommendation
- Next safe step
The recommendation matters.
An agent that only says "it depends" is not helping enough.
Yes, reality has nuance.
But at the end of the analysis, the agent should still tell you what it would do and why.
Then it should stop at the decision boundary unless you ask it to execute.
That last part is important.
DeepThink is not "think for a bit, then immediately start editing production."
It is a judgment step before action.
 
Why This Compounds
The real value of agent skills is not that they make one answer better.
It is that they make good behavior reusable.
Once DeepThink is saved as a skill, you do not have to re-explain your preferred decision process every time.
You can just say:
Use DeepThink.
And the agent knows the mode:
- slow down
- gather evidence
- challenge assumptions
- compare paths
- prefer reversible moves
- recommend clearly
- end with the next safe step
That is how agents start to feel less like chatbots and more like operators.
Not because they are always moving.
Because they know when not to.
 
The Takeaway
The future of agent work is not just faster execution.
It is better gear shifting.
Fast when the task is obvious.
Careful when the decision is expensive.
Skeptical when assumptions are weak.
Decisive when evidence is strong.
That is what DeepThink is for.
Slow down before expensive mistakes.
Move fast after the decision is grounded.
 
I built a Hermes skill called DeepThink.
It is a "slow mode" for AI agents before expensive decisions:
- architecture
- debugging
- security
- launch scope
- product direction
The goal is simple:
slow down before expensive mistakes, then move fast once the decision is grounded.","entities":{"urls":[{"text":"https://github.com/kyegomez/OpenMythos"},{"text":"https://gist.github.com/abundantbeing/7f74b251d4f774666923e6bdb1e2f3f7"},{"text":"https://abs.twimg.com/emoji/v2/svg/1f4ab.svg"}]},"cover_media":"3_2070562824980455425","preview_text":"Most AI agents have one dangerous default:
They move.
